[TOOL]: add_tasks(descriptions=["Create Package dimension (Abstract + Concrete Tags: Basic, Premium, Platinum)","Create Assumption Variables: Launch Date, Monthly Price, Initial Members, Growth Rate, Churn Rate","Create Calculation Variables: Active Members, Revenue","Verify model health"], explanation="Planning gym revenue model tasks") [TOOL]: GetInstructions(action="get", explanation="Get structure building instructions", parameters={"category":"build-structure"}) [TOOL_RESULT]: {"count":4,"success":true,"task_ids":["task-9","task-10","task-11","task-12"]} [TOOL_RESULT]: {"category":"build-structure","content":"# Build Model Structure\n\n## Purpose\nUse this workflow when creating or modifying the Model's Variable and Assumption structure.\n\n## Prerequisites\n- Model context is set (`ModelAction(action='set-current')`)\n- You understand the Model's purpose (what outputs are needed)\n- Retrieve `variables` and `assumptions` object instructions if you haven't already\n\n## Build Order\n\nBuilding a Model structure follows a bottom-up approach for inputs and top-down for planning:\n\n### 1. Plan Top-Down\nStart with the desired outputs and work backwards:\n- What are the final output Variables? (e.g., Revenue, Profit, Cash Flow)\n- What calculations produce those outputs?\n- What inputs (Assumptions) feed those calculations?\n- Are there Dimensions? (e.g., by Region, by Product)\n\n### 2. Create Tags and Dimensions (if needed)\nIf the Model uses Dimensions:\n- Create Abstract Tags for each Dimension (e.g., \"Region\", \"Product\")\n- Create Concrete Tags as children (e.g., \"UK\", \"France\" under \"Region\")\n- Concrete Tags reference their parent via `isATagIds`\n- Retrieve `tags` object instructions for API details\n\n### 3. Create Scenarios and Sensitivities (if beyond Base)\nIf the Model needs multiple Scenarios or Sensitivities:\n- Create additional Scenarios with `basedOnScenarioId` referencing Base or another Scenario\n- Create Sensitivities for variance analysis\n- Retrieve `scenarios` object instructions for API details\n\n### 4. Build Variables Bottom-Up\nCreate Variables from inputs to outputs:\n\n**a) Assumption Variables first:**\n- Create the Variable with `defaultFormulaType: Assumption`\n- Set `unitOption: Defined` and define the Unit with correct TimeType\n- Add `tagParents` for Dimensional Variables\n- ModelAssumptions are created automatically when providing inline assumption values via TimeSegments\n- OR create ModelAssumptions separately and link via TimeSegments\n\n**b) Calculation Variables next:**\n- Create with `defaultFormulaType: Calculation`\n- Define `defaultCalculation` with `formulaText` referencing previously-created Variables\n- Set `unitOption: Inferred` (default for Calculations) unless you need a specific Unit\n- For Dimensional Variables, add matching `tagParents`\n\n**c) Aggregation Variables (if needed):**\n- Create when different Dimension parts have different calculation logic\n- Set `aggregatedVariableIds` to reference the part Variables\n- Set `groupSummaryType` (usually `Sum`)\n\n### 5. Validate Structure\nAfter creating all Variables:\n- Check for unit warnings\n- Verify calculation dependencies resolve correctly\n- Use `get-details` on key Variables to confirm their computed values\n\n## Common Patterns\n\n### Simple Linear Model\nInput -\u003e Calculation -\u003e Output\n```\nPrice (Assumption) -\u003e Revenue = Price x Volume (Calculation) -\u003e Profit = Revenue - Costs (Calculation)\nVolume (Assumption) /\nCosts (Assumption) /\n```\n\n### Dimensional Model\nSame structure but with `tagParents` on Variables that need to expand across Dimensions.\n\n### Model with Time Flexibility (Rearranging)\nFor Actuals + Forecast models, see the Rearranging section in `variables` object instructions.\n\n## Key Rules\n- Assumption Variables must have `unitOption: Defined` with a TimeType on at least one UnitPart\n- Calculation Variables default to `unitOption: Inferred`\n- Each ModelSheet type supports only one kind of LineItem\n- Assumption-type Variables belong on Calculation sheets, not Assumption sheets\n","description":"Workflow for creating Variables, Assumptions, Tags, and Scenarios","title":"Build Model Structure","topics":["Variables","ModelAssumptions","Tags","Dimensions","structure","build"]} [ASSISTANT]: Good, I have the workflow. Now let me get the detailed object instructions for Tags, Variables, and Assumptions so I can create everything correctly. [TOOL]: GetInstructions(action="get", explanation="Get Tags/Dimensions instructions", parameters={"category":"tags"}) [TOOL]: GetInstructions(action="get", explanation="Get Variables instructions", parameters={"category":"variables"}) [TOOL]: GetInstructions(action="get", explanation="Get Assumptions instructions", parameters={"category":"assumptions"}) [TOOL_RESULT]: {"category":"tags","content":"# Working with Tags\n\n## Overview\n\nTags provide categorization, dimensional expansion, and unit definitions. The Abstract→Concrete Tag hierarchy is the foundation for Dimensions, Validation dropdowns, and Units.\n\n## Tag Types\n\n### Abstract Tags\n- Define categories/dimensions (e.g., \"Region\", \"Product\", \"Currency\")\n- Can serve as Dimensions when Variables reference them via `tagParents`\n- Have `concreteChildrenOrder` to control display order\n- Have `tagProperties` for advanced UnitPart control\n\n### Concrete Tags\n- Specific instances (e.g., \"UK\", \"France\", \"GBP\")\n- Reference their Abstract parent(s) via `isATagIds`\n- Become dimension members or dropdown options\n\n## Creating Tags for Dimensions\n\n### Step 1: Create the Abstract Tag\n```json\n{\n \"action\": \"create\",\n \"object_type\": \"Tag\",\n \"parameters\": {\n \"name\": \"Region\",\n \"tagType\": \"Abstract\"\n }\n}\n```\n\n### Step 2: Create Concrete Tags\n```json\n{\n \"action\": \"create\",\n \"object_type\": \"Tag\",\n \"parameters\": {\n \"name\": \"UK\",\n \"tagType\": \"Concrete\",\n \"isATagIds\": [\"-regionAbstractTagId\"]\n }\n}\n```\n\n### Step 3: Set Display Order\nUpdate the Abstract Tag's `concreteChildrenOrder` to control how dimension members appear:\n```json\n{\n \"action\": \"update\",\n \"object_type\": \"Tag\",\n \"parameters\": {\n \"id\": \"-regionAbstractTagId\",\n \"concreteChildrenOrder\": [\"-ukTagId\", \"-franceTagId\", \"-germanyTagId\"]\n }\n}\n```\n\n## Tags for Validation Dropdowns\n\nSame structure as Dimensions — create an Abstract Tag with Concrete children. Then set `validationDimensionId` on the Variable to the Abstract Tag ID, and set `dataType: Validation`.\n\nExample: \"Yes/No\" dropdown\n- Abstract: \"Yes/No Option\"\n- Concrete: \"Yes\", \"No\"\n\n## Tags for Units\n\nAbstract Tags also serve as the basis for UnitParts. A UnitPart references an Abstract Tag via `tagId`. Common examples:\n- \"GBP\" Abstract Tag → UnitPart for currency\n- \"Customer\" Abstract Tag → UnitPart for counting\n\n## UnitPartModifiers\n\nWhen creating dimensional Assumptions, the dimension context is encoded as modifiers on UnitParts. Each modifier references a Concrete Tag:\n\n```json\n{\n \"unit\": {\n \"unitParts\": [{\n \"tagId\": \"-gbpTagId\",\n \"timeType\": \"Flow\",\n \"modifiers\": [\n { \"tagId\": \"-ukConcreteTagId\" }\n ]\n }]\n }\n}\n```\n\nThis creates a UnitPart of \"GBP (UK)\" — encoding that this assumption belongs to the UK dimension member.\n\nFor multiple Dimensions, add multiple modifiers:\n```json\n\"modifiers\": [\n { \"tagId\": \"-ukConcreteTagId\" },\n { \"tagId\": \"-premiumConcreteTagId\" }\n]\n```\n\n## contextVarianceMap\n\nControls which UnitParts vary with which Dimensions. Structure:\n```json\n{\n \"contextVarianceMap\": {\n \"\u003cunitPartId\u003e\": [\"\u003cdimensionTagId1\u003e\", \"\u003cdimensionTagId2\u003e\"],\n \"\u003cunitPartId2\u003e\": [\"\u003cdimensionTagId1\u003e\"]\n }\n}\n```\n\n- **Keys**: UnitPart IDs from the Variable's Unit (use `get-details` on the Unit to find these)\n- **Values**: Abstract Tag IDs from the Variable's `tagParents`\n- **Default**: All UnitParts vary with all Dimensions (usually correct)\n- **Custom**: Only needed when different UnitParts in a ratio have different dimensional behavior\n\n### Example: Bonus Pot Percentage\nVariable \"Bonus Pot % by Region\" with Unit `GBP/GBP`:\n- Numerator GBP varies by Region (each region's share)\n- Denominator GBP does NOT vary (represents total pot)\n\n```json\n{\n \"contextVarianceMap\": {\n \"\u003cnumerator-gbp-unitpart-id\u003e\": [\"\u003cregion-tag-id\u003e\"],\n \"\u003cdenominator-gbp-unitpart-id\u003e\": []\n }\n}\n```\n\n## TagNamespaces\n\nModels can only reference Tags within their TagNamespace or descendant namespaces. When creating Tags for a Model, ensure they're in the correct namespace.\n\n## Best Practices\n\n1. Use descriptive names for Abstract Tags — they appear as dimension headers\n2. Set `concreteChildrenOrder` for predictable display ordering\n3. Use the same Abstract Tags for both Dimensions and Units when appropriate\n4. Only customize `contextVarianceMap` when the default \"all vary with all\" is incorrect\n","description":"Object reference: Tag creation, Abstract/Concrete setup, Dimensions, contextVarianceMap, UnitPartModifiers","title":"Working with Tags","topics":["Tag","Abstract","Concrete","Dimensions","UnitPartModifiers","contextVarianceMap","TagNamespace"]} [TOOL_RESULT]: {"category":"variables","content":"# Working with Variables\r\n\r\n## Overview\r\n\r\nVariables are the primary calculation containers in Models. This guide covers complex Variable properties and patterns that require deeper understanding beyond basic schema descriptions.\r\n\r\n## Targeted Editing (Partial Updates)\r\n\r\nVariable updates support targeted edits. Only include the properties you want to change. Omitted properties are preserved.\r\n\r\n- **Omitted property**: keeps the existing value\r\n- **Explicit null**: clears the value (when allowed by the schema)\r\n\r\nExample (update just the name):\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"id\": \"var-123\",\r\n \"name\": \"Updated Variable Name\"\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n## Key Concepts\r\n\r\n**Variable**: A calculation container with a `defaultFormulaType` that determines its behavior:\r\n- **Calculation**: Uses mathematical formulas referencing other Variables\r\n- **Assumption**: Gets values from ModelAssumption objects (user inputs)\r\n- **Aggregation**: Collects Variables representing Dimension parts into a Dimension-level Variable\r\n\r\n**Dimensions**: Abstract Tags with Concrete Tag children that expand Variables across multiple categories. When a Variable references an Abstract Tag via `tagParents`, it automatically expands to generate separate calculation lines for each Concrete child.\r\n\r\n## Defining Calculations with formulaText\r\n\r\n### Overview\r\n\r\nWhen creating or updating Calculation Variables, you can define the formula using `formulaText` - a human-readable string format - instead of the verbose `calculationParts` array.\r\n\r\n### Where to Use\r\n\r\nThe `formulaText` property can be used inside any `calculation` or `defaultCalculation` object:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"name\": \"Profit\",\r\n \"defaultFormulaType\": \"Calculation\",\r\n \"defaultCalculation\": {\r\n \"formulaText\": \"Revenue - Costs\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Syntax\r\n\r\n#### Variable References\r\n- Use the Variable name directly: `Revenue`\r\n- Quote names with special characters: `\"Revenue (Net)\"`\r\n- Quote names with spaces: `\"Total Revenue\"`\r\n\r\n#### Operators\r\n- Addition: `+`\r\n- Subtraction: `-`\r\n- Multiplication: `*`\r\n- Division: `/`\r\n- Exponentiation: `^`\r\n- Parentheses for grouping: `(Revenue - Costs) * 100`\r\n\r\n#### Constants\r\n- Numbers: `100`, `3.14`, `-5`\r\n- Negative values: `-100` or `0 - 100`\r\n\r\n#### Period Offsets\r\n- Previous period: `Revenue[-1]`\r\n- Two periods back: `Revenue[-2]`\r\n- Useful for growth calculations: `Revenue - Revenue[-1]`\r\n\r\n#### Period References\r\nThe `Periods` keyword returns the current period's end date as a date value. This is useful for creating timing flexibility, e.g. `Is Live = Periods \u003e Launch Date`\r\n\r\nKey points:\r\n- Returns the **end date** of the period (e.g., 31 Dec for a Calendar Year)\r\n- Supports offsets: `Periods[-1]` returns the previous period's end date\r\n- Compare against date-type Assumption Variables (Variables with date values)\r\n\r\nFinancial modeling best practice with Periods:\r\n- When flexible timing is required use Variables with DateType `Date` (Model periods don't have a time component)\r\n- It is always best to create a `Boolean` Variable for a timing condition (e.g. `Service is live`) and then to multiply that by the relevant Variable, rather than perform both timing logic and calculation in a single Variable\r\n\r\n#### Functions\r\n- Standard functions: `SUM(...)`, `MAX(...)`, `MIN(...)`, `IF(...)`\r\n- Example: `IF(Revenue \u003e 0, Profit / Revenue, 0)`\r\n\r\n### Examples\r\n\r\n**Simple calculation:**\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"defaultCalculation\": {\r\n \"formulaText\": \"Revenue - Costs\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n**With period offset (growth calculation):**\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"defaultCalculation\": {\r\n \"formulaText\": \"Revenue - Revenue[-1]\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n**With parentheses and constants:**\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"defaultCalculation\": {\r\n \"formulaText\": \"(Revenue - Costs) / Revenue * 100\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n**With quoted variable names:**\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"defaultCalculation\": {\r\n \"formulaText\": \"\\\"Net Revenue\\\" - \\\"Operating Costs\\\"\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n**With period reference (date comparison):**\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"defaultCalculation\": {\r\n \"formulaText\": \"Periods \u003e \\\"Launch Date\\\"\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Precedence Rules\r\n\r\n- If both `formulaText` and `calculationParts` are provided, `calculationParts` takes precedence\r\n- `formulaText` is converted to `calculationParts` before the Variable is created/updated\r\n- The API response includes `formulaText` generated from the stored `calculationParts`\r\n\r\n### Error Handling\r\n\r\nParse errors return structured error messages with:\r\n- Position in the formula where the error occurred\r\n- What was found vs. what was expected\r\n- Hints for resolution (e.g., \"Variable 'Unknown' not found in Model\")\r\n\r\n## Aggregation Variables\r\n\r\n### Critical Understanding: Aggregation is NOT Summing\r\n\r\n**Common Misconception**: Aggregation Variables \"sum\" other Variables.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: Aggregation Variables **collect** Variables that each represent a part of a Dimension into a Variable of that Dimension.\r\n\r\n### Example: Regional Revenues\r\n\r\nSuppose you have:\r\n- **Revenues-South**: Calculation Variable for southern region revenues\r\n- **Revenues-North**: Calculation Variable for northern region revenues\r\n- Both are Concrete Tags under the \"Region\" Abstract Tag (Dimension)\r\n\r\nYou can create:\r\n- **Revenues by Region**: Aggregation Variable that collects Revenues-South and Revenues-North\r\n\r\nThe Aggregation Variable expands to show:\r\n- Revenues by Region (South) = Revenues-South\r\n- Revenues by Region (North) = Revenues-North\r\n\r\nEach region may calculate revenues differently, but the Aggregation Variable provides a unified Dimension-level view.\r\n\r\n### When to Use Aggregation\r\n\r\n- When you have Variables representing Dimension parts (e.g., regions, products, markets)\r\n- When you want a Dimension-level Variable that combines these parts\r\n- When each part may have different calculation logic\r\n\r\n### Property: `aggregatedVariableIds`\r\n\r\n- **Type**: `array[string]` - Array of Variable IDs\r\n- **Relevant When**: `defaultFormulaType` is `Aggregation`\r\n- **Purpose**: Lists the Variables representing Dimension parts to collect\r\n- **Note**: These Variables must represent parts of the same Dimension(s) that the Aggregation Variable uses\r\n\r\n## contextVarianceMap\r\n\r\n### Purpose\r\n\r\nThe `contextVarianceMap` defines which unit parts vary with which Dimensions. This is critical for Variables with both:\r\n- **Dimensions** (via `tagParents`)\r\n- **Defined units** (via `unitOption: Defined`)\r\n\r\n### When It's Relevant\r\n\r\n- Variable has `unitOption: Defined` (not Inferred)\r\n- Variable has Dimensions (via `tagParents` with at least one parent)\r\n- **Both conditions must be true**\r\n\r\n### Structure\r\n\r\nThe map is a dictionary where:\r\n- **Key**: Unit part ID (e.g., \"GBP\", \"Member\", \"Month\")\r\n- **Value**: Set of Dimension (TagParent) IDs that this unit part varies with\r\n\r\n### Example\r\n\r\nSuppose a Variable has:\r\n- Unit: \"GBP per Member per Month\" (with UnitPart IDs: `up-gbp-123`, `up-member-456`, `up-month-789`)\r\n- Dimensions: \"Region\" (Tag ID: `tag-region-abc`) and \"Product\" (Tag ID: `tag-product-def`)\r\n\r\nThe default `contextVarianceMap` would be:\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"up-gbp-123\": [\"tag-region-abc\", \"tag-product-def\"],\r\n \"up-member-456\": [\"tag-region-abc\", \"tag-product-def\"],\r\n \"up-month-789\": [\"tag-region-abc\", \"tag-product-def\"]\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n**Important**: The map uses actual object IDs, not human-readable names:\r\n- **Keys**: UnitPart IDs from the Variable's Unit (use `get-details` on the Unit to find these)\r\n- **Values**: Tag IDs of the Dimensions from `tagParents` (Abstract Tag IDs)\r\n\r\nThis means all unit parts vary with all Dimensions (the default).\r\n\r\n### Custom contextVarianceMap\r\n\r\nYou only need to provide a custom `contextVarianceMap` when:\r\n- You want specific unit parts to vary with specific Dimensions only\r\n- The default \"all vary with all\" doesn't match your requirements\r\n\r\n**Important**: Each Dimension must vary with at least one unit part. Otherwise, there wouldn't be variance for that Dimension.\r\n\r\n### Property: `contextVarianceMap`\r\n\r\n- **Type**: `object` (dictionary mapping unit part IDs to Dimension ID sets)\r\n- **Relevant When**: `unitOption` is `Defined` AND Variable has Dimensions\r\n- **Default**: Automatically generated as \"all Dimensions vary with all unit parts\"\r\n- **Rarely Required**: Most Variables use the default map\r\n\r\n## groupSummaryType\r\n\r\n### Purpose\r\n\r\nControls how a Variable summarizes across all Dimensions when creating summary lines.\r\n\r\n### Values\r\n\r\n#### `None`\r\n- No automatic summary behavior\r\n- Variable appears only at the detailed Dimension level\r\n\r\n#### `Sum`\r\n- Always performs a simple sum across all Dimension items\r\n- Example: If you have Revenues by Region (South, North), Sum gives Total Revenues = South + North\r\n\r\n#### `Auto`\r\n- Performs the Variable's calculation on the summary lines of referenced Variables\r\n- Useful when the result differs from a simple sum\r\n- **Example**: Weighted average price\r\n - If Price = Revenue / Volume\r\n - Auto calculates: Total Price = Total Revenue / Total Volume\r\n - This is different from averaging individual prices\r\n\r\n#### `Rearranged`\r\n- Similar to Auto, but requires a parent Variable reference\r\n- Used when the summary calculation can only be computed from parent Variables\r\n- **Example**: Average price when Price is an input (not calculated)\r\n - If Price is an input Variable (not Revenue/Volume)\r\n - You need to rearrange: Price = Revenue / Volume\r\n - But Revenue and Volume are parent Variables\r\n - Rearranged uses `groupSummaryRearrangedParentId` to reference the parent for calculation\r\n\r\n### When to Use Each\r\n\r\n| Use Case | groupSummaryType | Notes |\r\n|----------|------------------|-------|\r\n| Simple totals | `Sum` | Direct sum of all Dimension items |\r\n| Calculated metrics | `Auto` | Recalculate formula on summary lines |\r\n| Input-based metrics | `Rearranged` | Calculate from parent Variables |\r\n\r\n### Property: `groupSummaryType`\r\n\r\n- **Type**: `enum` - `None`, `Sum`, `Auto`, `Rearranged`\r\n- **Default**: `None`\r\n- **Purpose**: Controls Dimension summary behavior\r\n\r\n### Property: `groupSummaryRearrangedParentId`\r\n\r\n- **Type**: `string` - Variable ID\r\n- **Required When**: `groupSummaryType` is `Rearranged`\r\n- **Purpose**: References the parent Variable to base the Rearranged summary calculation on\r\n- **Example**: For weighted averages requiring parent data\r\n\r\n## Unit System\r\n\r\n### Defined vs Inferred\r\n\r\n#### `unitOption: Defined`\r\n- Unit is explicitly set via the `unit` property\r\n- Used for Assumption and Aggregation Variables (default)\r\n- Can also be used for Calculation Variables\r\n\r\n#### `unitOption: Inferred`\r\n- Unit is automatically calculated from the formula\r\n- Only available for Calculation Variables\r\n- Based on the Variables referenced in the calculation\r\n- If unit cannot be calculated, a unit error is shown\r\n\r\n### Unit Factoring (`applyFactoring`)\r\n\r\n**Purpose**: Automatically adjusts calculations when the Variable's unit factor differs from the calculated unit factor.\r\n\r\n**How It Works**:\r\n- Factoring is done automatically based on units\r\n- Only works if `unitOption` is `Defined`\r\n- The Variable's unit must have a different factor from the calculated unit\r\n- Example: Variable has unit in millions (factorPower 6/1), but calculation returns thousands (factorPower 3/1)\r\n- Setting `applyFactoring: true` automatically adjusts the calculation (divides by 10^3)\r\n\r\n**Property**: `applyFactoring`\r\n- **Type**: `boolean`\r\n- **Default**: `false`\r\n- **Purpose**: Auto-adjust when Variable unit factor differs from calculated unit factor\r\n- **Note**: User doesn't need to multiply or divide within the Variable's calculation\r\n\r\n### Unit Validation\r\n\r\n- Automatic unit compatibility checking across Variable references\r\n- Unit inconsistencies generate warnings, not errors - calculations still execute\r\n- `overrideUnitError: true` allows bypassing unit validation when needed\r\n\r\n## TimeSegments\r\n\r\n### Purpose\r\n\r\nAllow Variables to change formula behavior across different time periods.\r\n\r\n### Types of TimeSegments\r\n\r\n#### Assumption TimeSegments\r\n- Provide values from ModelAssumption objects for specific periods/dimensions\r\n- Can override the default assumption for specific time ranges\r\n\r\n#### Calculation TimeSegments\r\n- Provide different calculation formulas for specific time periods\r\n- **Critical Constraint**: Calculation formulas within TimeSegments can only reference the same Variable with period offsets (e.g., `[-1]` for previous period)\r\n- They cannot reference other Variables to prevent circular dependencies\r\n\r\n### Creating Assumptions Inline with TimeSegments\r\n\r\nWhen creating a Variable with Assumption TimeSegments, you can provide assumption values directly as a 2D grid. This eliminates the need to create ModelAssumption objects separately.\r\n\r\n#### Grid Convention\r\n\r\n- **Columns**: Time periods (chronological order, determined by TimeSegment's date range)\r\n- **Rows**: Dimension cross-product (if Variable has dimensions via `tagParents`)\r\n- **Empty/null values**: Skip (no assumption created for that cell)\r\n\r\n#### Example: Simple Time Series (No Dimensions)\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"name\": \"Growth Rate\",\r\n \"defaultFormulaType\": \"Assumptions\",\r\n \"timeSegments\": [{\r\n \"start\": { \"segmentPeriodType\": \"Auto\" },\r\n \"duration\": { \"segmentDurationType\": \"PeriodsGeneric\", \"genericPeriods\": 6 },\r\n \"assumptions\": {\r\n \"values\": [\r\n [\"0.05\", \"0.06\", \"0.07\", \"0.08\", \"0.09\", \"0.10\"]\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n }]\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\nThe Variable's name (\"Growth Rate\") is automatically used as the assumption prefix.\r\n\r\n#### Example: Dimensional Variable (Region Dimension)\r\n\r\nFor a Variable with Region dimension (North, South) and 3 time periods:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"name\": \"Sales Growth\",\r\n \"defaultFormulaType\": \"Assumptions\",\r\n \"tagParents\": [{ \"tagId\": \"-regionDimensionId\" }],\r\n \"timeSegments\": [{\r\n \"start\": { \"segmentPeriodType\": \"Auto\" },\r\n \"duration\": { \"segmentDurationType\": \"PeriodsGeneric\", \"genericPeriods\": 3 },\r\n \"assumptions\": {\r\n \"values\": [\r\n [\"0.05\", \"0.06\", \"0.07\"],\r\n [\"0.04\", \"0.05\", \"0.06\"]\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n }]\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\nRow order is deterministic (ordered by TagGroupWithParents.OrderedId). Use `expand-dimensions` action to discover the exact grid shape.\r\n\r\n#### Grid Shape Discovery\r\n\r\nBefore providing values, you can use the `expand-dimensions` action to discover the expected grid shape:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"objectType\": \"Variable\",\r\n \"action\": \"expand-dimensions\",\r\n \"id\": \"var-123\",\r\n \"timeSegmentIndex\": 0\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\nReturns:\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"columns\": [\"2021\", \"2022\", \"2023\"],\r\n \"rows\": [\"North\", \"South\"],\r\n \"shape\": [2, 3]\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\nThis tells you:\r\n- The Variable expects a 2×3 grid\r\n- Rows are \"North\" then \"South\" (dimension cross-product)\r\n- Columns are \"2021\", \"2022\", \"2023\" (time periods)\r\n\r\n#### How It Works\r\n\r\nWhen you provide an `assumptions` object on a TimeSegment:\r\n1. The TimeSegment's formula type is automatically set to `Assumptions`\r\n2. After the Variable is created, ModelAssumption objects are generated from the grid\r\n3. Each grid cell creates one assumption with the correct Unit (including time and dimension modifiers)\r\n4. Assumptions are linked to the TimeSegment's formula\r\n5. Values use the Base Scenario by default\r\n\r\n#### When to Use\r\n\r\n- **Creating new Variables with Assumption TimeSegments**: Simplest approach\r\n- **Simple time series**: Just need to provide values across periods\r\n- **Dimensional assumptions**: Values vary by dimension and time\r\n\r\n#### When NOT to Use\r\n\r\n- **Updating existing assumptions**: Use direct ModelAssumption updates instead\r\n- **Multi-scenario values**: Use standard ModelAssumption creation with explicit values per scenario\r\n- **Complex sensitivity setups**: Create ModelAssumptions directly with sensitivity configuration\r\n\r\n### defaultAssumptionId\r\n\r\n**Purpose**: Fallback assumption for any period/dimensions in a TimeSegment that doesn't have a matching assumption.\r\n\r\n**When Used**:\r\n- Very rarely in practice\r\n- Typically, all assumption IDs are provided within TimeSegments\r\n- Example: If you want the Variable to have the value 0 except for periods that you provide a value for\r\n\r\n**Property**: `defaultAssumptionId`\r\n- **Type**: `string` - ModelAssumption ID\r\n- **Available For**: All formula types (even Calculation Variables can have Assumption TimeSegments)\r\n- **Purpose**: Fallback assumption for periods/dimensions without matching TimeSegment assumption\r\n\r\n### TimeSegment Coverage\r\n\r\nTimeSegments must collectively cover all periods without gaps or overlaps.\r\n\r\n## periodType\r\n\r\n### Purpose\r\n\r\nOverrides the Model's default period for this specific Variable.\r\n\r\n### Rules\r\n\r\n- Must be set to a broader time interval than the Model timings\r\n- Example: If Model is in weeks, you can define a Variable in months\r\n- `None` means \"use the Model's default period\"\r\n\r\n### Property: `periodType`\r\n\r\n- **Type**: `enum` - `None`, `Day`, `Week`, `Month`, `Quarter`, `YearCalendar`, `YearFiscal`\r\n- **Default**: `None` (use Model default)\r\n- **Purpose**: Override Model's default period for this Variable\r\n\r\n## validationDimensionId\r\n\r\n### Purpose\r\n\r\nDefines dropdown options for Validation-type Variables.\r\n\r\n### How It Works\r\n\r\n- Variable must have `dataType: Validation`\r\n- `validationDimensionId` references an Abstract Tag (Dimension)\r\n- The Concrete Tag children of that Abstract Tag become the dropdown options\r\n- Example: \"Yes/No\" Dimension with Concrete children \"Yes\" and \"No\"\r\n\r\n### Property: `validationDimensionId`\r\n\r\n- **Type**: `string` - Abstract Tag ID\r\n- **Required When**: `dataType` is `Validation`\r\n- **Purpose**: Abstract Tag defining dropdown options for Validation-type Variables\r\n\r\n## Common Patterns and Pitfalls\r\n\r\n### Pattern 1: Dimensional Aggregation\r\n\r\n**Scenario**: You have revenue calculations for each region, and want a total revenue Variable.\r\n\r\n**Correct Approach**:\r\n1. Create Calculation Variables: \"Revenues-South\", \"Revenues-North\" (each with Region Dimension)\r\n2. Create Aggregation Variable: \"Total Revenues\" with `aggregatedVariableIds: [\"Revenues-South\", \"Revenues-North\"]`\r\n3. Set `groupSummaryType: Sum` if you want summary lines\r\n\r\n**Incorrect**: Trying to sum the Variables in a Calculation formula - use Aggregation instead.\r\n\r\n### Pattern 2: Unit Factoring\r\n\r\n**Scenario**: Your Model works in thousands, but you want to display a Variable in millions.\r\n\r\n**Correct Approach**:\r\n1. Set Variable unit to millions (factorPower 6/1)\r\n2. Ensure calculation returns thousands (factorPower 3/1)\r\n3. Set `applyFactoring: true`\r\n4. System automatically adjusts (divides by 10^3)\r\n\r\n**Incorrect**: Manually dividing in the calculation formula.\r\n\r\n### Pattern 3: Weighted Averages\r\n\r\n**Scenario**: You want average price across all products.\r\n\r\n**If Price is Calculated** (Price = Revenue / Volume):\r\n- Use `groupSummaryType: Auto`\r\n- System recalculates: Total Price = Total Revenue / Total Volume\r\n\r\n**If Price is an Input**:\r\n- Use `groupSummaryType: Rearranged`\r\n- Set `groupSummaryRearrangedParentId` to reference Revenue or Volume parent\r\n- System rearranges calculation from parent Variables\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 1: Confusing Aggregation with Summing\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Thinking `aggregatedVariableIds` creates a sum.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: Aggregation collects Dimension parts, not sums values.\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 2: Missing contextVarianceMap Condition\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Providing `contextVarianceMap` when Variable has no Dimensions.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: `contextVarianceMap` is only relevant when Variable has both Dimensions AND Defined unit.\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 3: Wrong groupSummaryType\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Using `Sum` for calculated metrics that need recalculation.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: Use `Auto` or `Rearranged` when the summary result differs from a simple sum.\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 4: Self-Referencing Variable with Inferred Unit\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Creating a Calculation Variable that references itself with a period offset (e.g., `Balance[-1] + Inflow`) and leaving `unitOption: Inferred`.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: Self-referencing Variables require `unitOption: Defined`. The Unit system cannot resolve self-references to determine the inferred type. Always define the Unit explicitly with the appropriate TimeType (typically Balance for accumulator patterns).\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 5: Constant Inputs Producing Single-Value Output\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: A Calculation like `Price * Quantity` where both inputs are ConstantFlow produces only a single value instead of a time series.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: When all inputs to a Calculation are constant (ConstantFlow, ConstantBalance), the inferred Unit is also constant — meaning a single value for all periods. If you need time-varying output:\r\n1. Set `unitOption: Defined`\r\n2. Define a Unit with Flow or Balance TimeType (not ConstantFlow/ConstantBalance)\r\n3. This forces the Variable to calculate a value for each period\r\n\r\n## Best Practices\r\n\r\n1. **Use Aggregation for Dimension-based collection**, not for simple summing\r\n2. **Rely on default contextVarianceMap** unless you have specific requirements\r\n3. **Use Auto for calculated metrics** that need recalculation on summary lines\r\n4. **Use Rearranged only when necessary** - when calculation requires parent Variables\r\n5. **Set applyFactoring when unit factors differ** - don't manually adjust in formulas\r\n6. **Use defaultAssumptionId sparingly** - prefer explicit TimeSegment assumptions\r\n7. **Validate periodType compatibility** - must be broader than Model timings\r\n\r\n## Retrieving Calculated Values\r\n\r\n### get-values Operation\r\n\r\nRetrieve calculated values for one or more Variables:\r\n\r\n```\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Variable', action='get-values', parameters={\r\n 'ids': ['var-1', 'var-2'],\r\n 'periods': 'sample', # 'all', 'first', 'last', 'first-N', 'last-N', 'sample', or specific periods\r\n 'dimensions': 'summary', # 'summary', 'all', or specific paths like 'UK/Small'\r\n 'case': 'Base/Base', # Scenario/Sensitivity\r\n 'includeAnalysis': true # Adds trend, min/max, growth rate\r\n})\r\n```\r\n\r\n**Key considerations**:\r\n- Use `periods: 'sample'` for a quick snapshot (first, middle, last)\r\n- Use `dimensions: 'summary'` unless you need individual dimension values\r\n- The `includeAnalysis` option adds trend classification and growth rate\r\n\r\n### compare-cases Operation\r\n\r\nCompare values between two cases (Scenario/Sensitivity combinations):\r\n\r\n```\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Variable', action='compare-cases', parameters={\r\n 'ids': ['profit'],\r\n 'baseCase': 'Base/Base', # Required: baseline for comparison\r\n 'compareCase': 'Lean/Base', # Required: case to compare against baseline\r\n 'periods': 'last'\r\n})\r\n```\r\n\r\nReturns values with variance from baseline (absolute and percentage difference).\r\n\r\n### trace-dependencies with Values\r\n\r\nAdd `includeValues: true` to see values alongside the dependency tree:\r\n\r\n```\r\nModelAction(action='trace-dependencies', variableId='profit', includeValues=true, valuePeriod='last')\r\n```\r\n\r\n### valueSummary in Filter\r\n\r\nAdd `valueSummary` to filter properties to see first/last values and trend:\r\n\r\n```\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Variable', action='filter', parameters={\r\n 'properties': ['name', 'valueSummary']\r\n})\r\n```\r\n\r\nReturns `valueSummary: { first: { period, value }, last: { period, value }, trend: 'growing' }` for each Variable.\r\n","description":"Object reference: Variable properties, formulaText, CalculationParts, TimeSegments, DimensionReferences, Aggregation, Units, retrieving calculated values","title":"Working with Variables","topics":["Variables","Calculations","Aggregation","Dimensions","Units","TimeSegments","contextVarianceMap","groupSummary","DimensionReference","get-values","compare-cases","valueSummary"]} [TOOL_RESULT]: {"category":"assumptions","content":"# Working with ModelAssumptions\r\n\r\n## Overview\r\n\r\nModelAssumptions are user-input containers that provide values to Variables. They store scalar values that can vary by Scenario and Sensitivity. When a Variable has `defaultFormulaType: Assumptions`, it pulls its values from a ModelAssumption.\r\n\r\n## Key Concepts\r\n\r\n**ModelAssumption**: A container for user-input values with the following key properties:\r\n- **prefix**: The base name before dimensional/time context is added\r\n- **values**: A map of values keyed by Scenario ID\r\n- **sensitivities**: A map of sensitivity overrides keyed by Sensitivity ID\r\n- **sensitivityType**: The type of sensitivity calculation (PercentageChange, Delta, Absolute)\r\n- **unit**: The unit definition including any dimensional modifiers\r\n\r\n**Relationship to Variables**: Variables with `defaultFormulaType: Assumptions` reference a ModelAssumption via `defaultAssumptionId`. The Variable gets its calculated values from the assumption.\r\n\r\n## Values Map\r\n\r\nThe `values` property is a `Dictionary\u003cstring, ModelAssumptionValue\u003e` where:\r\n- **Keys**: Scenario IDs (e.g., `\"-Pt6e56nOUBefHIEvLbi\"`)\r\n- **Values**: ModelAssumptionValue objects\r\n\r\n### ModelAssumptionValue Structure\r\n\r\nEach value entry specifies:\r\n- **modelAssumptionValueType**: Currently only `LocalInput` is supported\r\n- **localInputValue**: The actual value as a string (parsed based on dataType)\r\n\r\n### Example\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-scenarioId1\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"29.99\"\r\n },\r\n \"-scenarioId2\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"34.99\"\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n**Important**: If a Scenario is \"based on\" another Scenario and has no explicit value, it inherits from its base Scenario.\r\n\r\n## Sensitivities Map\r\n\r\nThe `sensitivities` property is a `Dictionary\u003cstring, SensitivityValue\u003e` where:\r\n- **Keys**: Sensitivity IDs (e.g., `\"-Pt6e56nOUBefHIEvLbj\"`)\r\n- **Values**: SensitivityValue objects\r\n\r\n### SensitivityValue Structure\r\n\r\nEach sensitivity entry specifies:\r\n- **sensitivityValueType**: Must match the parent assumption's `sensitivityType`\r\n- One of: `percentage`, `delta`, or `absolute` (depending on type)\r\n\r\n### sensitivityType Matching\r\n\r\n**Critical**: The `sensitivityType` on ModelAssumption determines the type for ALL sensitivity values. Individual `SensitivityValue.sensitivityValueType` values must match.\r\n\r\n| sensitivityType | Required Field | Meaning |\r\n|-----------------|---------------|---------|\r\n| PercentageChange | percentage | Multiply base value by (1 + percentage/100) |\r\n| Delta | delta | Add delta to base value |\r\n| Absolute | absolute | Replace base value entirely |\r\n\r\n### Example\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"sensitivityType\": \"PercentageChange\",\r\n \"sensitivities\": {\r\n \"-sensitivityId1\": {\r\n \"sensitivityValueType\": \"PercentageChange\",\r\n \"percentage\": \"10\"\r\n },\r\n \"-sensitivityId2\": {\r\n \"sensitivityValueType\": \"PercentageChange\",\r\n \"percentage\": \"-5\"\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n## Dimensional Assumptions\r\n\r\nAssumptions can represent values across Dimensions (e.g., by Region, by Product). Unlike Variables which expand across Dimensions via `tagParents`, dimensional assumptions require **separate ModelAssumption objects** for each dimension combination.\r\n\r\n### How Dimensions Work\r\n\r\n1. **One Assumption per dimension combination**: For \"Price\" varying by Region (North, South) and Tier (Basic, Premium), you create 4 assumptions\r\n2. **Same prefix, different modifiers**: All share the same `prefix` but have different Unit modifiers\r\n3. **Modifiers on UnitParts**: Dimensional context is stored as `modifiers` on UnitPart objects\r\n\r\n### Example: New Members per Month by Region and Tier\r\n\r\nFor a model with:\r\n- Region Dimension: North, South\r\n- Tier Dimension: Basic, Premium\r\n\r\nYou would create 4 ModelAssumptions, all with `prefix: \"New members per month\"`:\r\n\r\n| Assumption | Value | Unit Modifiers |\r\n|------------|-------|----------------|\r\n| Assumption 1 | 30 | North, Basic |\r\n| Assumption 2 | 10 | North, Premium |\r\n| Assumption 3 | 5 | South, Basic |\r\n| Assumption 4 | 18 | South, Premium |\r\n\r\n### Unit Modifiers Structure\r\n\r\nEach UnitPart can have modifiers that specify dimensional context:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"unit\": {\r\n \"unitParts\": [\r\n {\r\n \"tagId\": \"-memberTagId\",\r\n \"modifiers\": [\r\n {\r\n \"tagId\": \"-northTagId\"\r\n },\r\n {\r\n \"tagId\": \"-basicTagId\"\r\n }\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\nThe `modifiers` array contains references to Concrete Tags that define the dimensional position of this assumption.\r\n\r\n## Time Series Assumptions\r\n\r\nAssumptions can represent values at specific points in time or over specific periods. This is achieved by adding a `dateTimeElement` to a UnitPart.\r\n\r\n### DateTimeElement Types\r\n\r\n| Element Type | Description | Use Case |\r\n|--------------|-------------|----------|\r\n| **Balance** | Point-in-time snapshot | Stock values (e.g., \"Price as of Dec 2024\") |\r\n| **Flow** | Period with duration | Flow values (e.g., \"Revenue for Q1 2024\") |\r\n| **ConstantBalance** | Time-invariant stock | Values that don't change over time |\r\n| **ConstantFlow** | Time-invariant flow | Rates that apply to all periods |\r\n\r\n**Important**: `ConstantBalance` and `ConstantFlow` do NOT require a `dateTimeElement`. Only `Balance` and `Flow` need one.\r\n\r\n### DateTimeSeriesDate Formats\r\n\r\nThe `dateString` format depends on the `dateType`:\r\n\r\n| dateType | Format | Example |\r\n|----------|--------|---------|\r\n| Day | `yyyyMMdd` | `\"20240115\"` (January 15, 2024) |\r\n| Week | `yyyyMMdd` | `\"20240121\"` (end of week, e.g., Sunday) |\r\n| Month | `yyyyMM` | `\"202401\"` (January 2024) |\r\n| Quarter | `yyyyQ[1-4]` | `\"2024Q1\"` (Q1 2024) |\r\n| Year | `yyyy` | `\"2024\"` |\r\n\r\n**Note**: Quarter and Year formats use calendar year conventions (Q1 = Jan-Mar, year ends December 31). The system will interpret these correctly based on the model's fiscal year settings.\r\n\r\n### Month-Specific Options\r\n\r\nWhen `dateType` is `Month`, you can specify when in the month:\r\n\r\n| monthDateType | Description |\r\n|---------------|-------------|\r\n| EndOfMonth | End of the specified month (default) |\r\n| DayInMonth | Specific day, requires `dayOfMonth` (1-28) |\r\n\r\n### DateTimeElement Structure\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"dateTimeElement\": {\r\n \"elementType\": \"Flow\",\r\n \"dateType\": \"Quarter\",\r\n \"dateString\": \"2024Q1\",\r\n \"duration\": 1\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\nFor Balance (snapshot) elements, omit `duration`:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"dateTimeElement\": {\r\n \"elementType\": \"Balance\",\r\n \"dateType\": \"Month\",\r\n \"dateString\": \"202412\",\r\n \"monthDateType\": \"EndOfMonth\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Pattern: Time Series Assumption\r\n\r\nA quarterly revenue assumption for Q1 2024:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelAssumption\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"prefix\": \"Revenue\",\r\n \"dataType\": \"Double\",\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-baseScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"1000000\"\r\n }\r\n },\r\n \"unit\": {\r\n \"unitParts\": [\r\n {\r\n \"tagId\": \"-revenueTagId\",\r\n \"timeType\": \"Flow\",\r\n \"dateTimeElement\": {\r\n \"elementType\": \"Flow\",\r\n \"dateType\": \"Quarter\",\r\n \"dateString\": \"2024Q1\",\r\n \"duration\": 1\r\n }\r\n }\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Pattern: Balance Snapshot Assumption\r\n\r\nA monthly balance (e.g., headcount at end of month):\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelAssumption\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"prefix\": \"Headcount\",\r\n \"dataType\": \"Integer\",\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-baseScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"150\"\r\n }\r\n },\r\n \"unit\": {\r\n \"unitParts\": [\r\n {\r\n \"tagId\": \"-headcountTagId\",\r\n \"timeType\": \"Balance\",\r\n \"dateTimeElement\": {\r\n \"elementType\": \"Balance\",\r\n \"dateType\": \"Month\",\r\n \"dateString\": \"202412\"\r\n }\r\n }\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Pattern: Unitless Time Series\r\n\r\nFor a unitless value at a specific time (no tag, just time context):\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelAssumption\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"prefix\": \"Growth Rate\",\r\n \"dataType\": \"Double\",\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-baseScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"0.05\"\r\n }\r\n },\r\n \"unit\": {\r\n \"unitParts\": [\r\n {\r\n \"tagId\": null,\r\n \"timeType\": \"Flow\",\r\n \"dateTimeElement\": {\r\n \"elementType\": \"Flow\",\r\n \"dateType\": \"Year\",\r\n \"dateString\": \"2024\",\r\n \"duration\": 1\r\n }\r\n }\r\n ]\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Time Series Pitfalls\r\n\r\n**Pitfall 1: Missing duration for Flow**\r\n\r\nFlow elements require a `duration` (minimum 1). Balance elements should not have duration.\r\n\r\n**Pitfall 2: Mismatched timeType and elementType**\r\n\r\nThe `timeType` on UnitPart should match the `elementType` in dateTimeElement:\r\n- `timeType: \"Balance\"` → `elementType: \"Balance\"`\r\n- `timeType: \"Flow\"` → `elementType: \"Flow\"`\r\n\r\n**Pitfall 3: Wrong date format**\r\n\r\nEach dateType has a specific format. Using `\"2024-01\"` instead of `\"202401\"` for Month will fail validation.\r\n\r\n## Common Patterns\r\n\r\n### Pattern 1: Simple Scalar Assumption\r\n\r\nA single value that doesn't vary by Scenario:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelAssumption\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"prefix\": \"Tax Rate\",\r\n \"dataType\": \"Double\",\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-baseScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"0.25\"\r\n }\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Pattern 2: Multi-Scenario Assumption\r\n\r\nValues that differ by Scenario:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelAssumption\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"prefix\": \"Revenue Growth Rate\",\r\n \"dataType\": \"Double\",\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-baseScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"0.05\"\r\n },\r\n \"-optimisticScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"0.08\"\r\n },\r\n \"-pessimisticScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"0.02\"\r\n }\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Pattern 3: Assumption with Sensitivities\r\n\r\nAdd sensitivity analysis capability:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelAssumption\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"prefix\": \"Discount Rate\",\r\n \"dataType\": \"Double\",\r\n \"sensitivityType\": \"Delta\",\r\n \"values\": {\r\n \"-baseScenarioId\": {\r\n \"modelAssumptionValueType\": \"LocalInput\",\r\n \"localInputValue\": \"0.08\"\r\n }\r\n },\r\n \"sensitivities\": {\r\n \"-highSensitivityId\": {\r\n \"sensitivityValueType\": \"Delta\",\r\n \"delta\": \"0.02\"\r\n },\r\n \"-lowSensitivityId\": {\r\n \"sensitivityValueType\": \"Delta\",\r\n \"delta\": \"-0.02\"\r\n }\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n## Common Pitfalls\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 1: Confusing Values Keys with Sensitivities Keys\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Using the wrong ID type as map keys.\r\n\r\n**Reality**:\r\n- `values` keys are **Scenario IDs**\r\n- `sensitivities` keys are **Sensitivity IDs**\r\n\r\nThese are different object types with different IDs.\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 2: Mismatching sensitivityType\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Setting `sensitivityType: PercentageChange` but providing `delta` values in sensitivities.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: The `sensitivityValueType` on each SensitivityValue must match the parent assumption's `sensitivityType`.\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 3: Expecting Dimensional Expansion\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Thinking one assumption can cover multiple dimension combinations.\r\n\r\n**Reality**: Each dimension combination requires its own ModelAssumption. Use Unit modifiers to differentiate them.\r\n\r\n### Pitfall 4: Numeric Values as Numbers\r\n\r\n**Mistake**: Providing numeric values as JSON numbers (`\"localInputValue\": 29.99`).\r\n\r\n**Reality**: All values are stored as strings. Always use string format (`\"localInputValue\": \"29.99\"`).\r\n\r\n## Best Practices\r\n\r\n1. **Use descriptive prefixes** that clearly identify what the assumption represents\r\n2. **Provide values for the Base Scenario** at minimum - other scenarios can inherit\r\n3. **Match sensitivityType with your analysis needs**:\r\n - Use `PercentageChange` for multiplicative sensitivity (e.g., revenue changes)\r\n - Use `Delta` for additive sensitivity (e.g., rate adjustments)\r\n - Use `Absolute` for replacement values\r\n4. **Create dimensional assumptions systematically** - ensure all combinations are covered\r\n5. **Query existing Scenarios and Sensitivities** before creating assumptions to get valid IDs\r\n","description":"Object reference: ModelAssumption properties, values maps, sensitivities, dimensional assumptions, unit modifiers","title":"Working with ModelAssumptions","topics":["ModelAssumption","Scenarios","Sensitivities","Dimensions","UnitModifiers","values"]} [ASSISTANT]: Now I have all the instructions I need. ## Changes Made
### 1. Fixed Launch Date - ✅ Value now correctly set to **01 Apr 25** - ✅ Number format changed to date format (`dd mmm yy`)
### 2. Simplified Active Members Calculation The formula is now much cleaner and easier to audit:
| Variable | Formula | |----------|---------| | **Active Members** | `Active Members[-1] + New Members - Churned Members` |
### 3. Split Out Member Movements
| Variable | Formula | Purpose | |----------|---------|---------| | **New Members** | `Is Live × (Active Members[-1] × Growth Rate + Initial Members if first live period)` | Shows member acquisition | | **Churned Members** | `Is Live × Active Members[-1] × Churn Rate` | Shows member losses |
### 4. Added Total Revenue - ✅ References the summary line of Revenue (sum across all packages)

The values look off:
1. **Jun 25** shows 170 new members (the initial members at launch) - that's correct 2. **But subsequent periods show only 3-4 new members total** - that seems far too low
The issue is that the growth rate is a **monthly** rate (3%, 2%, 1%), but the model runs on **quarterly** periods. I'm applying a monthly growth rate to a quarterly calculation without adjusting for the 3 months in each quarter.
For example, Basic has 3% monthly growth. With ~100 members, we'd expect roughly: - Monthly: 100 × 3% = 3 new members per month - Quarterly: Should be ~9 new members (compounded over 3 months), not 3

The quarterly compounding formula `(1 + monthly rate)^3 - 1` now correctly converts the monthly rates to quarterly equivalents: - **3% monthly growth** → ~9.3% quarterly growth - **5% monthly churn** → ~15.8% quarterly churn
The model shows that with the current assumptions, churn outpaces growth - which is realistic for the Basic package having high churn (5%/month) but only moderate growth (3%/month). The gym would need to either reduce churn or increase acquisition to grow membership over time.

## Final Model Structure
### Assumptions | Variable | By Package | Values | |----------|------------|--------| | Launch Date | No | 01 Apr 25 | | Monthly Price | Yes | Basic: £29.99, Premium: £49.99, Platinum: £79.99 | | Initial Members | Yes | Basic: 100, Premium: 50, Platinum: 20 | | Gross Additions | Yes | Basic: 15, Premium: 8, Platinum: 3 | | Monthly Churn Rate | Yes | Basic: 5.0%, Premium: 3.0%, Platinum: 2.0% |
### Calculations | Variable | Formula | |----------|---------| | Is Live | `Periods > Launch Date` | | New Members | `Is Live × (Gross Additions + Initial Members if first live period)` | | Churned Members | `Active Members[-1] × Quarterly Churn` | | Active Members | `Active Members[-1] + New Members - Churned Members` | | Revenue | `Active Members × Monthly Price × 3` | | Total Revenue | Sum of Revenue across packages |

Four assumptions, six calculations, one timing gate. 👍 [USER]: Please check for errors, then create a dashboard [TOOL]: ModelAction(action="get-model-health", explanation="Check for model errors", parameters={}) [TOOL]: GetInstructions(action="get", explanation="Get dashboard building instructions", parameters={"category":"build-visualisations"}) [TOOL_RESULT]: {"errors":[{"category":"variableCalculation","id":"-Pa-5aC-9ncWVDAey1J3","message":"Expecting `Member [Flow]`, calculated `Member [Constant Flow]`","name":"New Members"}],"warnings":[]} [TOOL_RESULT]: {"category":"build-visualisations","content":"# Build Visualisations\n\n## Purpose\nUse this workflow when creating Dashboards, Charts, Metrics, or Tables.\n\n## Prerequisites\n- Model context is set\n- Model structure and calculations exist\n- Retrieve `dashboards` object instructions for API details if you haven't already\n\n## Workflow\n\n### 1. Determine What to Visualise\n- **KPI values** → Create Metrics + add to Dashboard\n- **Trends over time** → Create Charts\n- **Scenario comparison** → Create Tables\n- **Editable inputs** → Create AssumptionsTables\n\n### 2. Create Metrics (if needed)\nMetrics produce single-value outputs from Variables. Required for:\n- Dashboard KPI displays\n- Table inputs (data table comparison)\n\nKey considerations:\n- Must force a single time value (specify a period or aggregate: SUM, MAX, etc.)\n- Can be Dimensional (only time is restricted)\n- Get `dashboards` object instructions for Metric calculation patterns\n\n### 3. Create Dashboard\nA Dashboard is a container for DashboardItems:\n- **Panel**: Container for grouping items\n- **Chart**: Single Variable visualisation\n- **Metric**: Single value display\n- **Table**: Multi-Case comparison (Excel data table)\n- **AssumptionsTable**: Editable assumption inputs\n- **Spacer**: Visual spacing\n\n### 4. Configure Charts\nFor each Chart:\n- Select the Variable to chart\n- Choose 2 of {Time, Dimensions, Scenarios, Sensitivities} for Categories and Series\n- Configure filter values for remaining axes (not interactive — set specific values)\n- Select chart type (Column, Line, Stacked Column, etc.)\n\n### 5. Create Tables (for Scenario Comparison)\nTables compare Metric values across Cases:\n1. Create the Metrics you want to compare\n2. Create a Table referencing those Metrics\n3. Add the Table to a Dashboard\n4. Create a Dashboard ModelSheet for that Dashboard\n\n### 6. Add to Layout\nFor Excel output, Dashboards need a Dashboard ModelSheet:\n- Create a ModelSheet with type `Dashboard`\n- Reference the Dashboard ID\n- **Layout constraint:** If Dashboard has an AssumptionsTable, those Assumptions can't have entry cells elsewhere in the Layout\n\n### 7. Validate\n- Run `ModelAction(action='validate-layout')` to check for Layout conflicts\n- Review Dashboard in app for visual correctness\n","description":"Workflow for creating Dashboards, Charts, Metrics, and Tables","title":"Build Visualisations","topics":["Dashboard","Chart","Metric","Table","visualisation"]} [ASSISTANT]: There's a unit error on New Members - let me fix that first by overriding the unit warning (it's calculating correctly, just a unit inference issue from multiplying by the boolean Is Live).
[TOOL]: QueryObject(action="update", explanation="Override unit warning on New Members", object_type="Variable", parameters={"id":"-Pa-5aC-9ncWVDAey1J3","overrideUnitError":true}) [TOOL]: add_tasks(descriptions=["Create Metrics for dashboard KPIs","Create Dashboard with charts and metrics","Verify dashboard"], explanation="Plan dashboard creation") [TOOL_RESULT]: {"success":true,"variable":{"calculatedValues":{"growthRate":"0.0% per period","sample":{"Dec 27":"26","Mar 25":"0","Sept 26":"26"},"trend":"volatile"},"calculation":"Is Live * Gross Additions [Auto]","defaultFormulaType":"Calculation","formulaText":"Is Live * Gross Additions","id":"-Pa-5aC-9ncWVDAey1J3","name":"New Members","numberFormat":"#,##0","timeSegments":"`Mar 25 - Dec 27`: Auto","unit":"Member [Flow]","usedCalculationFormulaText":"Is Live * Gross Additions"}} [TOOL_RESULT]: {"count":3,"success":true,"task_ids":["task-19","task-20","task-21"]} [TOOL]: GetInstructions(action="get", explanation="Get dashboard object instructions", parameters={"category":"dashboards"}) [TOOL_RESULT]: {"category":"dashboards","content":"# Working with Dashboards\r\n\r\n## Overview\r\n\r\nDashboards provide in-app and Excel visualization. A Dashboard contains DashboardItems arranged in a layout.\r\n\r\n## DashboardItem Types\r\n\r\n| Type | Purpose |\r\n|---|---|\r\n| **Panel** | Container for grouping items (has child items) |\r\n| **Chart** | Single Variable visualisation |\r\n| **Metric** | Single value KPI display |\r\n| **Table** | Multi-Case comparison (Excel data table) |\r\n| **AssumptionsTable** | Editable assumption inputs |\r\n| **Spacer** | Visual spacing |\r\n\r\n## Charts\r\n\r\n### One Variable Per Chart\r\nEach Chart displays a single Variable. For multi-Variable comparison, create separate Charts.\r\n\r\n### Axis Configuration\r\nChoose any 2 of the following for **Categories** (x-axis) and **Series** (legend/color):\r\n- **Time**: The Model's time periods\r\n- **Each Dimension**: If the Variable has tagParents (e.g., Region, Product)\r\n- **Scenarios**: Compare across Scenarios\r\n- **Sensitivities**: Compare across Sensitivities\r\n\r\nThe remaining axes become **filters** — configure specific values during chart setup. Filters are NOT interactive in the rendered Dashboard; you select the specific filter values when creating the chart.\r\n\r\n### Chart Types\r\nColumn, Stacked Column, Line, Area, Bar, etc.\r\n\r\n### Example: Revenue by Region and Tier\r\nVariable \"Revenue\" has Dimensions: Region, Tier\r\n\r\n```\r\nCategories: Tier (x-axis shows: Basic, Premium)\r\nSeries: Region (colors show: UK, France)\r\nFilters: Time = \"2025\" (fixed to specific period)\r\n```\r\n\r\nThis creates a stacked column chart showing revenue breakdown by tier and region for 2025.\r\n\r\n## Metrics\r\n\r\n### Single-Value Outputs\r\nMetrics produce a single value from a Variable. You MUST force a single time value:\r\n\r\n**Option A: Specific period**\r\nReference a specific period in the Metric calculation.\r\n\r\n**Option B: Aggregate operation**\r\nUse SUM, MAX, MIN, AVERAGE etc. across the time range.\r\n\r\n### Dimensional Metrics\r\nMetrics CAN be dimensional (the time dimension is the only one restricted). A dimensional Metric will show values for each dimension member.\r\n\r\n### Common Patterns\r\n- \"Total 5-year Revenue\" → SUM aggregate across all periods\r\n- \"Exit Year EBITDA Margin %\" → Reference last period\r\n- \"NPV\" → Calculation using discount rate across periods\r\n\r\n### Retrieving Metric Values\r\n\r\nUse `get-values` to retrieve calculated Metric values:\r\n\r\n```\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Metric', action='get-values', parameters={\r\n 'ids': ['total-revenue', 'exit-margin']\r\n})\r\n```\r\n\r\nCompare across Cases (two modes):\r\n```\r\n# Multi-case mode: tabular view of values across cases (no variance)\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Metric', action='compare-cases', parameters={\r\n 'ids': ['total-revenue'],\r\n 'cases': ['Base/Base', 'Lean/Base', 'Base/High']\r\n})\r\n\r\n# Pairwise mode: detailed variance between two cases\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Metric', action='compare-cases', parameters={\r\n 'ids': ['total-revenue'],\r\n 'baseCase': 'Base/Base',\r\n 'compareCase': 'Lean/Base'\r\n})\r\n```\r\n\r\nAdd `valueSummary` to filter results for quick value context:\r\n```\r\nQueryObject(object_type='Metric', action='filter', parameters={\r\n 'properties': ['name', 'valueSummary']\r\n})\r\n```\r\n\r\n## Tables (Data Tables)\r\n\r\nTables compare Metric values across multiple Cases (Scenario + Sensitivity combinations).\r\n\r\n### Required Workflow\r\n1. **Create Metrics** that produce the values to compare\r\n2. **Create a Table** referencing those Metric IDs\r\n3. **Add the Table to a Dashboard** as a DashboardItem\r\n4. **Create a Dashboard ModelSheet** referencing that Dashboard\r\n\r\nThe Table generates an Excel data table that calculates each Metric for every specified Case combination.\r\n\r\n## AssumptionsTables\r\n\r\nDisplay editable Assumptions within a Dashboard. Key constraint:\r\n- Each Assumption can only have ONE entry cell in Excel\r\n- If a Dashboard with an AssumptionsTable is in the Layout, those Assumptions' entry cells move to the Dashboard sheet\r\n- The original Assumptions sheet will point to the Dashboard\r\n\r\n## Adding Dashboards to Layout\r\n\r\nFor Excel output, create a Dashboard ModelSheet:\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"action\": \"create\",\r\n \"object_type\": \"ModelSheet\",\r\n \"parameters\": {\r\n \"name\": \"Dashboard\",\r\n \"modelSheetType\": \"Dashboard\",\r\n \"dashboardId\": \"-dashboardId\"\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n## Best Practices\r\n\r\n1. Put Tables in their own Dashboard (one Table per Dashboard)\r\n2. Group related Charts and Metrics in Panels\r\n3. Configure chart filters to show the most relevant default view\r\n4. Always validate layout after adding Dashboard sheets\r\n5. Remember: chart filters are set during configuration, not interactive in the dashboard\r\n","description":"Object reference: DashboardItem properties, Chart axis config, Metric period handling, Table workflow, retrieving Metric values","title":"Working with Dashboards","topics":["Dashboard","Chart","Metric","Table","AssumptionsTable","DashboardItem","get-values","compare-cases"]} [ASSISTANT]: Let me get the schemas for creating Metrics and Dashboards.

## Gym Revenue Model - Final Summary
### Assumptions (4) | Variable | By Package | Values | |----------|------------|--------| | Launch Date | No | 01 Apr 25 | | Monthly Price | Yes | Basic: £29.99, Premium: £49.99, Platinum: £79.99 | | Gross Additions | Yes | Basic: 15, Premium: 8, Platinum: 3 | | Monthly Churn Rate | Yes | Basic: 5.0%, Premium: 3.0%, Platinum: 2.0% |
### Calculations (6) | Variable | Formula | |----------|---------| | Is Live | `Periods > Launch Date` | | New Members | `Is Live × Gross Additions` | | Churned Members | `Active Members[-1] × Quarterly Churn` | | Active Members | `Active Members[-1] + New Members - Churned Members` | | Revenue | `Active Members × Monthly Price × 3` | | Total Revenue | Sum of Revenue across packages |
### Dashboard: Gym Performance Dashboard
**Key Metrics:** | Metric | Value | |--------|-------| | Total Revenue (All Time) | £153,525 | | Final Active Members | 162 | | Final Quarter Revenue | £21,657 |
**Charts:** - Total Revenue Over Time - Active Members Over Time - Revenue by Package
